perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Minnesota.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Minnesota to California introduces potential conflicts, as California has specific regulations regarding trade secrets and non-disclosure agreements that may differ from Minnesota law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law, specifically CUTSA, governs trade secret protection and may impose different standards for what constitutes a trade secret and the permissible duration of non-disclosure obligations than Minnesota law, potentially affecting the enforceability of the agreement.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modification changes the governing law from Minnesota to California. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation describe that California law (specifically CUTSA) has specific regulations regarding trade secrets and non-disclosure agreements that may differ from Minnesota law. Snippet 1 states, "This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act." This suggests that California law provides specific protections and regulations regarding trade secrets, and by changing the governing law, the contract introduces a contradiction because the enforceability and interpretation of the agreement are now subject to a different legal standard. This contradiction falls under the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition because the deadline and requirements for trade secret protection change, which may violate state law.

perturbation.original_text: NEBS agrees not to buy Premium Wear's common shares without the written consent of the Board of Directors of Premium Wear prior to March 1, 2002.
perturbation.changed_text: NEBS agrees not to buy Premium Wear's common shares without the written consent of the Board of Directors of Premium Wear prior to January 1, 2001.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the standstill date to January 1, 2001, from March 1, 2002, affects the duration of the restriction on NEBS's ability to acquire Premium Wear's shares. If this restriction violates antitrust laws by unduly restraining trade for an unreasonable period, it could be deemed illegal.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the timeframe itself might seem innocuous, prematurely shortening the standstill period to January 1, 2001 could have competitive implications. Should it be proven that this enforced restraint of trade causes anti-competitive effects, such as artificially inflating prices due to lack of potential acquirers, or prevents other beneficial market dynamics, it could be viewed in conflict with the Sherman Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 15 U.S. Code § 1 - Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1 - Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty U.S. Code Notes prev | next Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal. Every person who shall make any contract or engage in any combination or conspiracy hereby declared to be illegal shall be deemed guilty of a felony, and, on conviction thereof, shall be punished by fine not exceeding $100,000,000 if a corporation, or, if any other person, $1,000,000, or by imprisonment not exceeding 10 years, or by both said punishments, in the discretion of the court. (July 2, 1890, ch. 647, § 1, 26 Stat. 209 ; Aug. 17, 1937, ch. 690, title VIII, 50 Stat. 693 ; July 7, 1955, ch. 281, 69 Stat. 282 ; Pub. L. 93–528, § 3 , Dec. 21, 1974 , 88 Stat. 1708 ; Pub. L. 94–145, § 2 , Dec. 12, 1975 , 89 Stat. 801 ; Pub. L. 101–588, § 4(a) , Nov. 16, 1990 , 104 Stat. 2880 ; Pub. L. 108–237, title II, § 215(a) , June 22, 2004 , 118 Stat. 668 .) Editorial Notes Amendments 2004— Pub. L. 108–237 substituted “$100,000,000” for “$10,000,000”, “$1,000,000” for “$350,000”, and “10” for “three”. 1990— Pub. L. 101–588 substituted “$10,000,000” for “one million dollars” and “$350,000” for “one hundred thousand dollars”. 1975— Pub. L. 94–145 struck out from first sentence two provisos granting anti-trust exemption to State fair trade laws. 1974— Pub. L. 93–528 substituted “a felony, and, on conviction thereof, shall be punished by fine not exceeding one million dollars if a corporation, or, if any other person, one hundred thousand dollars, or by imprisonment not exceeding three years” for “a misdemeanor, and on conviction thereof, shall be punished by fine not exceeding f
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text changes the standstill date, potentially creating an illegal restraint of trade. The original text prevents NEBS from buying Premium Wear's shares until March 1, 2002, whereas the changed text moves this date to January 1, 2001. This inconsistency could violate 15 U.S. Code § 1, which declares illegal any contract, combination, or conspiracy in restraint of trade. The law explanation suggests shortening the standstill period could lead to anti-competitive effects. The scraped law snippet directly states that 'Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal.' Thus, the change, by potentially restraining trade for an unreasonable period, contradicts federal law.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party will return all originals, copies, reproductions and summaries of Confidential Information immediately upon the disclosing party's request or, at the disclosing party's option, destroy the same, and will deliver to the disclosing party, upon its request, a certificate of a duly authorized officer certifying as to the foregoing.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party will return all originals, copies, reproductions and summaries of Confidential Information immediately upon the disclosing party's request or, at the disclosing party's option, destroy the same. No certificate of destruction will be required.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for a certificate of destruction could conflict with regulations regarding data security and compliance, particularly in industries with strict data protection laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts Standards for the Protection of Personal Information of Residents of the Commonwealth
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Massachusetts law, 201 CMR 17.00, companies handling personal information of Massachusetts residents must implement comprehensive information security programs. If the Confidential Information contains personal data of Massachusetts residents, eliminating the certificate of destruction could be seen as a failure to adequately document and verify the secure disposal of this information, thereby violating the spirit and intent of the law. This regulation requires businesses to take reasonable steps to ensure the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of personal information, which includes properly destroying or disposing of records containing such information and maintaining documentation of these actions.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for a certificate of destruction, which contradicts the intent of Massachusetts law 201 CMR 17.00, as explained by the LLM. This law requires companies to maintain comprehensive information security programs when handling personal information of Massachusetts residents. The absence of a certificate, which is a form of documentation, could be viewed as a failure to adequately document and verify the secure disposal of information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this by stating that 201 CMR 17.00 establishes minimum standards to ensure the security and confidentiality of customer information and protect against security breaches. Removing the certificate requirement weakens the documentation of secure disposal, potentially conflicting with this standard.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of each receiving party hereunder with respect to the non-disclosure and use of the disclosing party's Confidential Information shall survive until such time as all such Confidential Information disclosed hereunder becomes publicly known and made generally available through no action or inaction of the receiving party.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of each receiving party hereunder with respect to the non-disclosure and use of the disclosing party's Confidential Information shall cease after one (1) year from the date of disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the confidentiality obligation to one year could contradict trade secret law if the information retains its value and secrecy beyond that period.
perturbation.law_explanation: By limiting the confidentiality period to one year, the modified text potentially conflicts with the DTSA, which protects trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. If the information disclosed qualifies as a trade secret and retains its value and secrecy beyond the one-year period, the contractual limitation could be deemed unenforceable or a violation of trade secret law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA

Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The changed text limits the confidentiality period to one year, which directly contradicts the purpose of trade secret law (as explained in the Perturbation and Law Explanations). Trade secrets are protected as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage, which can extend far beyond one year. Snippet 1, referencing 18 U.S. Code § 1836, allows an owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated to bring a civil action, implying ongoing protection. The one-year limit weakens that protection and creates an inconsistency, because it could allow misappropriation after one year without recourse, even if the information remains a trade secret. Snippet 2 is not helpful.
